coredata.dc:title: New insights into mechanism of enhancing biogas residue dewaterability using carbon quantum dots and biochar composites under visible light through electrochemical approach

coredata.dc:description:
This study investigated the treatment of biogas residue using a catalytic activation system with carbon quantum dots/biochar (CQDs/BC) and H2O2, driven by visible light. Results indicated that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of CQDs/BC was 74.5 % lower than that of CQDs, with improved charge separation rates and enhanced light absorption. In the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system, relative capillary suction time (CST/CST0) and water content of biogas residue cake (Wc) decreased by 56.6 % and 8.9 % compared to control groups, respectively. CQDs/BC generated photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) under visible light, converted oxygen species, including ·OH, ·
O
2
-
, and 1O2, and considerably improved the oxidizing capacity of the system. The Rct of the treated biogas residue was 62.2 % lower than that of the original biogas residue and showed a significant correlation with Wc (r = 1.0, p < 0.001). These findings underscored that biogas residue Rct can be used as an indicative parameter of its dewatering performance.

head.ce:title: New insights into mechanism of enhancing biogas residue dewaterability using carbon quantum dots and biochar composites under visible light through electrochemical approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
This study investigated the treatment of biogas residue using a catalytic activation system with carbon quantum dots/biochar (CQDs/BC) and H2O2, driven by visible light. Results indicated that the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of CQDs/BC was 74.5 % lower than that of CQDs, with improved charge separation rates and enhanced light absorption. In the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system, relative capillary suction time (CST/CST0) and water content of biogas residue cake (Wc) decreased by 56.6 % and 8.9 % compared to control groups, respectively. CQDs/BC generated photogenerated electrons (e−) and holes (h+) under visible light, converted oxygen species, including ·OH, ·
O
2
-
, and 1O2, and considerably improved the oxidizing capacity of the system. The Rct of the treated biogas residue was 62.2 % lower than that of the original biogas residue and showed a significant correlation with Wc (r = 1.0, p < 0.001). These findings underscored that biogas residue Rct can be used as an indicative parameter of its dewatering performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: According to statistics from the National Bureau of Statistics of China, the amount of domestic waste removal increased from 155.093 million tons in 2004 to 244.447 million tons in 2022 (Statistics, 2022), with food waste contributing 50 %–65 % to this (Yan et al., 2024). Anaerobic digestion (AD) has emerged as a primary method for treating food waste, offering a cost-effective solution for renewable energy production and waste treatment (Xu et al., 2018). However, the residues from AD, known as food waste digestate (FWD), still require further treatment (Dutta et al., 2021). The challenge of dewatering FWD, which retains significant water content even after conventional mechanical conditioning, remains a critical bottleneck in effectively managing and disposing of biogas residue (Zhang et al., 2024a).

The extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) in biogas residue is considered to be the key substance to determine the dewaterability of biogas residue (Zhang et al., 2024a). Composed mainly of proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids, EPS binds water tightly within flocs, preventing its release into the free water phase (Yu et al., 2021a). Thus, disrupting EPS is crucial for promoting the release of bound water and improving dewatering performance. In recent years, free radicals (such as hydroxyl radicals, sulfate radicals, superoxide radicals, and ozone radicals) generated by advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) have been widely employed in the dewatering of sludge and biogas residues owing to their ability to degrade EPS structures with high water retention (Ling et al., 2022a). However, single AOPs often require substantial chemical inputs, complicating the balance between dewatering efficiency and energy conservation. Consequently, the concentration of free radicals in the system is critical for improving dewatering efficiency. Previous research has focused on increasing the production of free radicals by activating oxidants with metal ions (Ling et al., 2022a; Ling et al., 2021a), but residual metal ions have ecological risks. Biochar (BC), as a pollution-free and inexpensive activator, is widely used in sludge dewatering (Tao et al., 2019). This is because BC, which contains persistent free radicals such as aromatic radicals and hemiquinones (Luo et al., 2019), can activate H2O2 to produce hydroxyl radicals (Zhu et al., 2002).

Moreover, the application of photocatalytic oxidation in AOPs has garnered interest in pollution prevention (Cai et al., 2023), as certain photosensitizers can transfer electrons to O2 to generate superoxide radicals or singlet oxygen upon light excitation (Eq. 2). Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are widely employed in various fields including electrocatalysis (Ali et al., 2022), photocatalysis (Guo et al., 2023), and cell imaging (McMahon et al., 2024) due to their excellent conductivity, low toxicity, and unique optical and photovoltaic properties. CQDs feature amorphous or nanocrystalline cores primarily composed of sp2 carbon, exhibiting graphite-like lattice spacing and surface oxygen containing functional groups. CQDs demonstrate excellent photocatalytic activity due to effective charge separation and minimized electron-hole recombination (Guo et al., 2024). Their application in photocatalysis often involves integrating CQDs with other materials to boost efficiency (Chen et al., 2023; Guo et al., 2023). The primary oxidizing agents in this process are hydroxyl and superoxide radicals generated during illumination, as illustrated in Eqs. (1) and (2).
H2O+h+→·OH+H+(1)
O2+e−→·O2 −(2)

In this study, the effectiveness of CQDs and CQDs/BC as photocatalytic materials for treating biogas residue was compared, and their impact on dewatering performance and underlying mechanisms was investigated. The relationship between electrochemical properties and dewaterability of biogas residue was investigated, and the key free radicals in the system were identified by electron paramagnetic resonance. This study proposed a mechanism by which the conversion of visible light by CQDs/BC synergizes H2O2 to enhance biogas residue dewaterability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Materials
ce:section.ce:para:
Food waste biogas residue was obtained from Shanghai Renewable Energy Co., LTD. The samples collected from the anaerobic digester were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C. Citric acid, anhydrous ethanol, nafion, acetylene black, sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), bromo-naphthalene, formamide, bovine serum protein, sodium hydroxide, sodium carbonate, potassium sodium tartrate, Folin phenol, anthrone, concentrated sulfuric acid, and glucose were purchased from Shanghai Sinopath Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. DMPO, TEMPO, and TEMP were used as trapping agents for singlet oxygen, superoxide free radicals, hydroxyl free radicals, and holes, respectively. Tert-butanol (TBA), ammonium oxalate (AO), L-histidine (L-His), 1,4-benzoquinone (1.4-B) served as quenching agents for hydroxyl radicals (·OH), holes (h+), singlet oxygen (1O2), and superoxide radicals (·
O
2
-
), respectively. All chemicals were of analytical grade.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental methods
Preparation of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized using a one-step hydrothermal method (Wang et al., 2017; Zhao and Chung, 2018). First, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water and ultrasonicated for 10 min. This mixture was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and hydrothermally processed for 6 h at 200 °C. The supernatant was then filtered through a 0.22 μm hydrophilic membrane and dialyzed in a 3500-Da dialysis bag for 24 h to obtain the CQD solution. Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to collect the CQD powder.

BC was produced from corn stover by drying it at 105 °C for 24 h, followed by grinding to a particle size of less than 1 mm, and then the material was carbonized at 700 °C for 2 h (Zhao et al., 2021). For the preparation of CQDs/BC composites, mixed powders were weighed in mass ratios of CQDs:BC of 0:1, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, and 4:1 and pyrolyzed at 550 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dewatering experiment of biogas residue
ce:section.ce:para: Four series of batch experiments were conducted in beakers to evaluate the feasibility of CQDs/BC materials for degrading EPS macromolecular fractions under light and H2O2 conditions, aiming to enhance FWD dewatering performance. The main parameters optimized included the CQDs:BC doping ratio, CQDs/BC addition, H2O2 addition, and pH levels. The pH of 200 mL of FWD was adjusted to a predetermined value (pH = 3–10) using NaOH (1 M) and H2SO4 (3 M). The CST of the treated biogas residue and the water content of the biogas residue cake (Wc) were measured.

Based on the optimal values of the four variables identified in the previous experiments, seven additional sets of batch experiments were conducted (see Supplementary Materials). Treatments included acid-treated BC, CQDs, CQDs/BC, H2O2, and CQDs/BC + H2O2. The experimental conditions were optimized based on the results from the initial four sets of experiments, with FWD under the same light conditions serving as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical method
ce:section.ce:para: Malvern Nano-ZS90 zeta potential meter,Partica-LA-960 V2 laser particle size meter, pH meter, portable conductivity meter. Water content was determined via the gravimetric method. The contact angle of the biogas residue was assessed using a DH-HV1351UM device. A three-electrode system was utilized, featuring a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode and a platinum network counter electrode, with 0.2 mol/L Na2SO4 serving as the electrolyte. The photoelectric performance tests conducted in this study included photocurrent density (i-t), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and Mott–Schottky tests. The samples were analyzed using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The required plots for the experiment were generated using Origin 2021 software.

Ultraviolet/visible absorption was measured using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan), while fluorescence spectra were obtained with a Dutta fluorescence and absorption spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan). Raman spectra were acquired using the Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted with a Thermo Scientific K-alpha (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) utilized the Rigaku SmartLab SE (Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) recordings were performed with the Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20 (USA) to characterize molecular structures. Additionally, the surface morphology of the biogas residue was directly observed using high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (SEM) with a TESCAN MIRA LMS. The AFM and TEM images of the photocatalyst materials were tested by a Bruker Dimension Icon and a Japanese JEOL JEM-2100F instrument.

Soluble EPS (S-EPS), loosely bound EPS (LB-EPS), and tightly bound EPS (TB-EPS) were extracted using centrifugation, ultrasound, and water bath methods. Total organic carbon (TOC) in the EPS components was measured with a TOC analyzer (Shimadzu-GL). Protein concentration was determined using the Lowry (Folin) method, with bovine serum protein as the standard. Polysaccharide concentration was assessed via the phenol sulfuric acid method, using glucose as the standard. Three-dimensional excitation emission matrix measurements were conducted using the three-dimensional scanning mode of a fluorescence spectrophotometer. All experiments were conducted in triplicate. Spearman’s correlation coefficient was used to estimate the correlation between two variables. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) tests was carried out to show whether the results had a statistical difference. Datum was analyzed by using SPSS statistical software (SPSS version 19.0, SPSS Inc.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
Surface characteristics analysis of photocatalytic materials
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the synthesized CQDs were agglomerated spherical and relatively uniform in size, but the surface of the CQDs/BC showed an obvious pore structure. The number of lattices on the surface of CQDs was more than that of CQDs/BC and the lattice spacing increased to 0.812 nm, corresponding to the carbon structure associated with aromatic rings (002). Contrastingly, bulk CQDs possess a thickness reaching up to 15.5 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 1(b). Surprisingly, the CQDs/BC composite exhibits a thickness of 2.6 nm. The ultrathin nanosheet structure of CQDs/BC is expected to endow it with an extensive specific surface area, thereby exposing a multitude of photocatalysis active sites (Guo et al., 2024).

As shown in Fig. 1(c), the peaks at 3270 cm−1 and 3450 cm−1 in the CQDs/BC material were attributed to O-H stretching vibrations. The noticeable shift of these vibration peaks indicated that the dewatering reaction during high temperature pyrolysis cleaved hydrogen bonded hydroxyl groups in alcohols and carboxylic acids (Moradi-Choghamarani et al., 2019). Additionally, two main peaks at 1050 cm−1 and 1530 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C–O groups in alkyl ethers and C=C groups in sp2 hybridized planes, respectively. Weak peaks in the 2000–3000 cm−1 range represented C–H groups in aliphatic functional groups. This suggested a gradual disintegration of the aliphatic structure, a reduction of C–H groups in the aromatic structure, and an increase in C=C structure due to the introduction of graphitic carbon in BC. The increase in graphite, nitrogen, and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CQDs/BC contributed to variation in surface states, the formation of surface defects, and enhanced fluorescence intensity.

To further analyze the microstructure and estimate the level of lattice disorder in the two materials, Raman spectra in the range of 1000 to 1800 cm−1 were recorded. As shown in Fig. 1(d), two prominent peaks were identified: the D band at 1380 cm−1 and the G band at 1580 cm−1. The D peak was associated with defects and disordered scattering within the aromatic structure (Tagliaferro et al., 2020), corresponding to a the disordered sp3 configuration, while the G peak reflected the conjugated sp2 lamellar structure indicative of graphitization. Notably, a blue shift of the CQDs/BC peaks suggests the formation of a conjugate structure between CQDs and BC, which facilitates electron transfer (Rajender et al., 2018). The ratio of D peak area to G peak area (ID/IG) serves as a parameter of structural defects. The peak areas of the D and G peaks were derived by fitting the peaks to the Raman spectrum, and the peak areas were used to calculate the ID/IG (Shi et al., 2018). The ID/IG value for CQDs/BC was 47.65, higher than that of CQDs (which was 4.54), and higher than the ID/IG of monolayer graphene (2.51) and CQD (3.15) (Shi et al., 2018), indicating an increase in the proportion of defective condensed aromatic ring structures. The increase in defects within the graphite structure enhances the electron count through the π electron system, thereby improving the conductivity of CQDs/BC (Yang et al., 2024). This ratio indicated the presence of partially disordered crystals composed of small sp2 clusters. XRD measurements of CQDs/BC (see Supplementary Materials), revealed characteristic peaks at 2
θ
= 23.4° and 42.3°. Corresponding to the (002) planes (Biswas et al., 2022) of the conjugated sp2 carbon structure associated with aromatic rings and (533) (Saikia et al., 2020) planes, respectively. The addition of pyrolytic BC contributes to the composite’s stability due to its aromatic properties. These findings confirm that CQDs/BC functions effectively as both an electron donor and acceptor, enabling efficient electron-hole separation and demonstrating high catalytic activity as a photocatalytic material.

From the XPS analysis patterns of CQDs and CQDs/BC, the major variation of the two materials is concentrated in the C and N forms (see Supplementary Materials). The peak carbon intensity in CQDs/BC is significantly higher than that in CQDs, in which the form of C is transformed from C–OH/C–O to aromatic C–C/C=O (Guo et al., 2020a), which proves that the degree of graphitization is improved. The N in CQDs/BC transitions from quaternary N or NO groups (401.8 eV) to C–N–C/H (400.7 eV) and C–N=C (398.6 eV) in the organic matrix (Yang et al., 2019; Zou et al., 2020) (see Supplementary Materials). It has been previously reported that the degree of graphitization and the presence of C = O and C-N groups affect the emission center, light energy Eg and quantum yield of CQDs, which are all key factors affecting the optical properties of CQDs (Mei et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of photochemical properties of photocatalytic materials
ce:section.ce:para: Mott-Schottky plots of the CQDs and CQDs/BC were generated using the capacitance values at 800 Hz and 1600 Hz to determine their conduction band positions. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the positive slopes of the Mott-Schottky plots indicate that both materials exhibit typical N-type semiconductor properties (Zhang et al., 2024b). The flat-band potentials (Efb ) of CQDs and CQDs/BC are −0.68 eV and −0.8 eV, respectively. The conduction band (CB) values of N-type semiconductors are typically Efb -0.1/0.2 eV compared to Efb , and 0.2 eV is considered, thus yielding CB values of −0.88 eV and −1.0 eV for CQDs and CQDs/BC, respectively. Based on the equation E VB = E CB + E g (Zhang et al., 2024b), the values of valence bands are calculated to be −0.07 eV for CQDs and −0.25 eV for CQDs/BC, respectively. The corresponding energy band structures were plotted according to the obtained values. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the Eg of CQDs/BC was narrowed from 0.81 eV to 0.75 eV compared with that of CQDs, to be lower than the Eg value of 3.16 for the CQDs prepared with a mixture of chitosan and graphite (Shi et al., 2018), which agreed with the results of the photoluminescence spectrogram of CQDs/BC demonstrated in Fig. 2(b), showing the red-shift phenomenon accompanied by the reduction of the Eg . The narrower the Eg , the wider the visible light absorption range and the higher the light utilization (Zhu et al., 2024). In addition, the CQDs/BC exhibited higher anodic current peaks (see Supplementary Materials), which demonstrated the higher light utilization and electron transfer rate (Yang et al., 2024; Zhu et al., 2024).

To further investigate the transient photocurrent response, photoelectrochemical measurements were performed under visible light irradiation for multiple switching cycles. The results (Fig. 2(c)) showed that the higher photocurrent intensity of the CQDs/BC was attributed to the abundant π-electronic properties in the CQDs/BC, which stimulated n-π* and π-π* electron leaps (Guo et al., 2024). In addition, as shown in Fig. 2(d), the EIS semicircle of CQDs/BC is smaller than that of the unmodified CQDs, indicating a decrease in the charge transfer resistance (Rct ) and an acceleration of charge carrier separation. All these results demonstrate that the close interaction between CQDs and BC accelerates the charge transfer while suppressing the rapid recombination of photogenerated e--h+, and that the effectiveness of photoinduced charge separation and transfer significantly impacts the photoelectrochemical characteristics of the materials (Guo et al., 2024).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variation of dewatering property of biogas residue during photocatalytic reaction
Optimization of photocatalytic reaction conditions
ce:section.ce:para: The CST and Wc were 422.13 ± 14.72 s and 80.72 ± 0.55 %, respectively, for a 2:1 doping ratio of CQDs to BC (see Supplementary Materials). This improvement in dewatering performance can be attributed to BC acting as a “skeleton builder,” which reduces slurry compressibility and creates a structural framework that facilitates water passage (Guo et al., 2020b). However, excessive small particle size CQDs clog the BC’s pore structure, which impedes water flow and deteriorates dewatering performance. Thus, a 2:1 ratio was selected for further experiments. On this basis, we also determined that the best dewatering performance of the biogas residue was achieved when the dosage of CQDs/BC was 0.5 g/L, the dosage of H2O2 was 0.06 g/gVS, and the pH was 3 (see Supplementary Materials). The results indicated that CQDs/BC effectively enhanced the oxidation capacity of H2O2, leading to the production of more reactive species. This increased activity disrupts biogas cells and network structures, resulting in greater intracellular water release and promoting the dewatering of biogas residue. Consequently, the improved dewatering properties of the biogas slurry were associated with a reduction in bound water within the biogas residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs/BC + H2O2 on dewatering performance of biogas residue
Comparative analysis of different treatments to improve the dewatering performance of biogas residue
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the optimal values of the four variables identified in the previous experiments, seven additional sets of batch experiments were conducted (see Supplementary Materials). The effects of various treatments on the dewatering efficiency of FWD under light conditions were analyzed.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a) and (b), the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system has the best performance of biogas residue dewatering with Wc and CST/CST0, respectively, which is attributed to the protonation of carboxylic acids in the EPS constituents under acidic condition, raising the zeta potential and neutralizes the negative charge on the EPS surface in the biogas residue. At the same time, CQDs/BC promote the generation of electron-hole pairs under light conditions (Ali et al., 2022), which can be converted to hydrogen peroxide and reactive oxygen radicals (Formulas 1 and 3), further enhancing the oxidizing ability of the system, destroying the dense EPS structure (Yu et al., 2021b), and ultimately facilitating the drainage of water (Zhang et al., 2024a).
O2+2e−+2H+→H2O2(3)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs/BC + H2O2 on physicochemical properties of biogas residue
ce:section.ce:para: Zeta potential plays a crucial role in controlling the stability of colloids, significantly influencing the flocculation and dewatering performance of biogas residue (Liu et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 3(c), the variation in zeta potential for systems with BC, CQDs, and CQDs/BC was minimal, indicating that oxidation was not activated. However, in systems with H2O2 and pH adjustment, the zeta potential increased from − 20.73 ± 1.29 in the control group to − 15.30 ± 1.86 and − 13.50 ± 2.19, respectively. This increase is due to H2O2 oxidation, which removed anionic groups from the EPS components, thus reducing the overall negative charge (Ling et al., 2021b). Under acidic conditions, the introduction of H+ ions further enhanced zeta potential by compressing the double electric layer and neutralizing negative charges. A protonation reaction occurred in the organic matter, decreasing surface charge density. A reduced negative charge weakens electrostatic repulsion, promoting the flocculation and precipitation of colloidal particles (Zhang et al., 2024a). Studies indicate that a lower absolute zeta potential leads to greater particle condensation and compression, as the electrostatic repulsion becomes weaker than the attractive forces, creating an unstable colloidal state that facilitates aggregation and dewatering (Dong et al., 2021). According to DLVO theory, this increase reduced the polarity between biogas residue particles and water, converted bound water into free water, and enhanced the separation of biogas residue particles from water and ultimately improved dewatering performance.

The results presented in Fig. 3(e) revealed that various sized voids were present on the surface of biogas residue before and after treatment, which enhanced its porosity and permeability. This indicates that the catalysts and oxidants create additional pathways for water release during dewatering (Zhen et al., 2012). The protonation of functional groups on EPS may decrease electrostatic repulsion, promoting flocculation and improving dewatering outcomes. The particle size results for biogas residue and control group in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system were presented (Fig. 3d). The average and median particle sizes (D50) of biogas residue flocculants decreased from 69.27 μm and 24.89 μm to 60.92 μm and 16.96 μm, respectively, indicating a more severe disintegration of the flocculants. This disintegration occurs because free radicals with strong oxidizing capabilities decompose EPS components, and damage the flocculant network and reducing particle size (Li et al., 2020). Consequently, the overall particle size of the biogas slurry becomes more uniform, leading to improved filtration performance and reduced moisture in the Wc. In contrast, the average and median particle sizes (D50) for the treatment system with H2O2 were 80.03 μm and 30.12 μm, respectively, both larger than those of the biogas residue in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system. This suggests that the CQDs/BC as catalysts significantly enhances the oxidation capacity of H2O2, facilitating the disintegration of flocculant structures and the formation of smaller fragments. Furthermore, CQDs/BC + H2O2 promotes the aggregation of small particles due to the flocculation effect, ultimately improving filtration performance.

In general, the hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity and the interface affinity of biogas flocs can be characterized by the contact angle (Yu et al., 2021b). The BC group exhibited a maximum contact angle of 78.57 ± 4.30° (see Supplementary Materials), indicating a significant increase in hydrophobicity due to the addition of BC, a strong hydrophobic material, which altered the overall hydrophilic and hydrophobic characteristics of the mixture (Guo et al., 2020b). In comparison, the CQDs/BC group had a contact angle of 73.65 ± 0.84°, notably higher than the control group’s angle of 59.94 ± 1.42°. However, the dewatering performance of both groups was not optimized. In contrast, the cleavage of the EPS structure releases some hydrophilic substances in the two groups containing the oxidizer H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2. The final contact angles for these groups were 56.51 ± 2.35° and 65.76 ± 1.17°, respectively. This indicates that the CQDs/BC + H2O2 effectively alters the hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties of the biogas residue surface, thereby enhancing its dewatering performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The composition and morphological variations of extracellular polymeric substances in biogas residue treated by different methods
Variations in total organic carbon in extracellular polymeric substances
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate variation in biopolymers within the EPS layer of biogas residue, S-EPS, LB-EPS, and TB-EPS were extracted from biogas residue samples. The TOC content of S-EPS in all groups was higher than that in the control group, except for the acid treatment group (see Supplementary Materials). In the CQDs/BC + H2O2 group, the TOC content in S-EPS was 4.06 ± 0.02 mg/g TS, which was 87 % higher than the control group (2.18 ± 0.02 mg/g TS), indicating that more organic matter was decomposed into the liquid phase following strong oxidation. An analysis of the characteristics of hydrophilic functional groups in S-EPS was conducted to further study the distribution of hydrophilic and hydrophobic components in biogas residue flocculates. The hydrophilic functional groups in S-EPS were identified using XPS C1s spectroscopy. Based on these results, carbon (C) was the primary element in EPS, categorized into hydrophilic C (C–OH and C = O) and hydrophobic C (CHX and C–CH3). The binding energies for different carbon functional groups are 283.2 eV for CHX, 284.8 eV for C–CH3, 286.3 eV for C–OH, and 288.0 eV for C = O. XPS analysis of the carbon morphology in S-EPS (see Supplementary Materials) revealed three main functional groups (Sarlaki et al., 2024) −[C-C/C-CH3], [C-OH/C-O], and [C = O] which correspond to aromatic substances, carbohydrate-like substances, and carboxylic acid substances, respectively. A peak near 292.0 eV indicated the presence of the CF2 bond. After treatment with the H2O2 group and the CQDs/BC + H2O2 group, alkyl and aromatic substances in the organic matter of biogas residue decreased, while the proportion of carbohydrate-like compounds increased, with little variation in the carbonyl group. In contrast, biogas residue S-EPS treated without oxidizing agents exhibited no significant alterations, as treatments (pH, BC, CQDs, CQDs/BC) that did not involve strong oxidation showed minimal degradation of organic matter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Variations of protein and polysaccharide in extracellular polymeric substances
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 (a), proteins were predominantly concentrated in S-EPS, with total protein levels in the three layers of both H2O2 supplemented groups significantly decreasing. Notably, the S-EPS protein in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 group exhibited the most pronounced decline. In the H2O2 only system, LB-EPS and TB-EPS protein concentrations dropped from 713.53 mg/L and 337.06 mg/L to 166.47 mg/L and 37.06 mg/L, respectively. In the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system, these concentrations were 84.12 mg/L and 31.18 mg/L, respectively. The BW showed significant positive correlations with LB-PN (r = 0.50, p < 0.05) and TB-PN (r = 0.50, p < 0.05), indicating that oxidatively released intracellular material contributes to protein degradation and increase in bound water release. The Wc was also positively correlated with S-PN (r = 1.0, p < 0.001) and LB-PN (r = 1.0, p < 0.001), as the loose protein structure of S-EPS and LB-EPS facilitates water release. Fig. 4(b) illustrated that the evolution of polysaccharide content and distribution in S-EPS contrasts with that of proteins; polysaccharide content increased in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system with higher oxidation capacity. S-PS was significantly negatively correlated with Wc (r = −1.0, p < 0.001). These results indicated that in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system, insoluble organic matter in EPS was degraded into soluble polysaccharides, contributing to the significant increase in S-TOC content in S-PS (r = 1.0, p < 0.001).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum analysis of extracellular polymeric substances
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence intensity of the two main peaks in EPS significantly decreased or even disappeared in both H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 treatment systems compared to the control group (see Supplementary Materials). This aligned with the decreasing trend in protein concentration observed in EPS components (Fig. 4(a)), suggesting that advanced oxidation effectively removes these hydrophilic compounds. This disruption may facilitate the removal of hydrogen bonds in water and peptide bonds in proteins, and thereby enhance the dewatering performance of biogas residue (Yu et al., 2021a). However, the variation in fluorescence intensity of S-EPS does not correspond with the polysaccharide concentration, likely due to the influence of polysaccharide state and molecular structure on fluorescence characteristics. These results indicate that the S-EPS component is critical in influencing the dewatering ability of biogas residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different treatment methods on Fourier transform infrared of biogas residue
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 4(c)) revealed that the O–H bonds in carbohydrates and N-H bonds in proteins exhibited the strongest tensile vibrations (3430 cm−1) in the control biogas residue. After treatment, this vibration peak decreased, particularly in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 group, indicating that the –OH bonds in the biogas residue were removed during oxidation. The reduction of hydroxyl groups weakened the hydrophilicity of the biogas residue, facilitated the separation and removal of water (Zhang et al., 2024a; Zhu et al., 2023). The FTIR spectra of biogas residue in the BC, CQDs, CQDs/BC, and pH groups showed no significant variation. In contrast, the H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 treatments led to decreased vibration intensities for C-H (2920 cm−1) in aliphatic structures, C=O (1654 cm−1) in the amide Ⅰ region, and N–H (1540 cm−1) in the amide Ⅱ region. This reflected that powerful free radicals could attack the hydrophilic functional groups of proteins and polysaccharides in the solid phase of biogas slurry (Ling et al., 2022b), and reduce the binding energy of bound water and hydrophilic organic matters. This promoted the conversion of bound water into free water (Wu et al., 2017). Additionally, the vibration peak at 1020 cm−1 in the H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 treatment groups shifted, with enhanced transmission, indicating an increase in the relative contents of C–OH and C–O–C related to polysaccharides. Previous studies have shown (Yu et al., 2021b) that the degradation of large EPS components occurs progressively, with hydrophilic substances in LB-EPS and TB-EPS being degraded and transferred to S-EPS. This phenomenon could be supported well by the similar change trends in the concentrations of PN and PS after treatment (Fig. 4(a) and (b)). Therefore, it could be concluded that H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 systems exhibited different degradation modes for the polysaccharides and proteins in EPS components, with simultaneous destruction of protein structures and stepwise degradation of polysaccharides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism analysis of photoelectric reaction promoting dewatering property of biogas residue
ce:section.ce:para: From Fig. 5 (a), the peak current of CV loops both H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 systems was strengthened. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the addition of H2O2 significantly reduced the R ct from an initial 28296 ± 113 to 11773 ± 425 Ω. This reduction indicated that the active substances generated by H2O2 accelerated the breakdown of the biogas residue floc structure and intensified electron transfer within the oxidation system (Choong et al., 2021). In the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system, Rct further decreased to 10586 ± 342 Ω, and the addition of CQDs/BC led to an increase in the number of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and free radicals within the system (Eqs. (1), (2), (3)) (Yu et al., 2021a), thereby enhancing the oxidation capacity of the system. The redox active functional groups in BC further intensified electron transfer (Eqs. (4), (5), (6)) (Xu et al., 2021). The lower Rct indicated higher conductivity (r = −1.0, p < 0.001) (Xu et al., 2021). In Fig. 5(c), conductivity was distinctly elevated from 20.83 ± 0.06 mS/cm to 28.53 ± 0.06 mS/cm under the addition of CQDs/BC + H2O2 as compared with raw biogas residue. The result proved that CQDs/BC’s utilization of visible light increased the frequency of electron transfer capacity, further promoted electrical conductivity and floc structure degradation (Choong et al., 2021). The increase in electrical conductivity confirmed that the charge flow capacity in the system was enhanced and led to more intense electrochemical reactions and the release of strong oxidizing free radicals (Zhang et al., 2019). This process promoted the cytolysis efficiency of biogas residue, increasing organic matter content in the liquid phase and releasing internal bound water (Jiang et al., 2023).

Rct was significantly positively correlated (r = 1.0, p < 0.001) with PN in all three EPS structures (see Supplementary Material). And it was determined that the variation of PN within the structures of S-EPS, LB-EPS, and TB-EPS significantly influenced the Wc of the biogas residue (r = 1.0, p < 0.001) (see Supplementary Material). It was observed that the Rct of the biogas residue system exhibited a significant positive correlation with the Wc (r = 1.0, p < 0.001) (Fig. 5(d)). The result further proved that the Rct can effectively serve as an indicative parameter of the dewatering performance of the biogas residue.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of free radical content and its contribution
ce:section.ce:para:
To elucidate the potential mechanism by which CQDs/BC materials enhance the degradation of large components under light irradiation, the active substances produced during the reaction were analyzed. The experiment was initially conducted under dark conditions and then exposed to visible light for 10 min and 30 min. Strong free radical signals emerged after light irradiation (Fig. 6 (a)), with intensity increasing over time, confirming the production of ROS. Moreover, the EPR signal intensity for superoxide radicals (·
O
2
-
) and singlet oxygen (1O2) in both systems increased and then diminished after 10 min. In contrast, the EPR signal intensity for hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and holes (h+) in the CQDs/BC + H2O2 system was significantly higher than in the H2O2 system, suggesting that CQDs/BC enhanced visible light capture, promoted photogenerated charge separation, and increased hole content (Guo et al., 2024). Additionally, BC acts as an activator, providing sufficient active sites for H2O2 to facilitate the generation of hydroxyl radicals (Xu et al., 2021). As a result, the oxidation capacity of the CQDs/BC + H2O2 group is much greater than that of the H2O2 group.

Additionally, free radical quenching experiments were conducted to assess the contribution of free radicals to the dewatering of biogas residue (Fig. 6(b) and (c)). In both H2O2 and CQDs/BC + H2O2 systems, the CST and Wc peaked at maximum values (1715.26 ± 12.38 s and 86.31 ± 0.32 %; 1593.70 ± 8.93 s and 84.47 ± 0.27 %) after the addition of tert-butanol (excluding the control group). The results indicated that ·OH radicals played a major role in both systems. The results demonstrated that the contribution to the filtration and dewatering capacities of biogas residue follows this order: ·OH > h+ > 1O2 > ·
O
2
-
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: This study demonstrated the potential of CQDs/BC synergized with H2O2 in improving the biogas residue dewaterability. CST/CST0 and Wc were reduced by 56.6 % and 8.9 % respectively compared to the control. CQDs/BC as the photocatalyst enhanced electron transfer capacity and strengthened the conductivity of oxidation in electrochemical treatment. The Rct value of the treated biogas residue was reduced by 62.6 % compared to the control, and a significantly positive correlation was also found between Rct and Wc of biogas residue (r = 1.0, p < 0.001). The Rct of biogas residue can be used as an indicative parameter for Wc.
